source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 11.3.1-11.3.2 managing services (pp. 202-205)

questions.why: systemctl start <name> starts the service; the .service suffix is optional.
systemctl stop <name> stops a running service.

questions.q: Check the status of the sshd service.
questions.why: systemctl status shows whether the service is running and recent log lines.

questions.q: What is the difference between 'systemctl start' and 'systemctl enable'?
options: start runs the service NOW; enable makes it start automatically at boot
start enables at boot; enable runs it now
start is permanent; enable is temporary
questions.why: start affects the current session only; enable creates the boot-time activation (and disable removes it).

questions.q: Configure the httpd service to start automatically at boot.
questions.why: systemctl enable activates the service at boot; disable turns that off.

questions.q: List the currently loaded service units.
questions.why: list-units --type service shows loaded services; list-unit-files --type service shows all and whether they're enabled.

questions.q: In a .service unit file, which three sections appear (as in the postfix example)?
questions.why: [Unit] holds description/ordering (After=, Conflicts=), [Service] the exec commands (ExecStart=), [Install] the WantedBy= target.

questions.q: In the postfix .service file, what does 'WantedBy=multi-user.target' in [Install] mean?
options: When enabled, the service is wanted by (started with) the multi-user.target
It sets the service's PID file
questions.why: WantedBy in [Install] ties the service to a target, so enabling it hooks it into that target's startup.
